title: Evangelion and wish fulfilment
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Wed May 09, 2012 8:37 am From what I've read about Hideaki Anno and his attitudes to "Otaku" culture, the purpose of Evangelion was to be a sort of anime that made people wake up and think instead of retreating into fantasy. This is why, amongst other things, fan-pleasing things such as characters "getting together" and happy endings do not happen in Evangelion. So, supposing, for example, a fan produces a fanfiction in which they rewrite the events of the story so that Shinji and friends survive and the character pairings they prefer happen, isn't that really a pointless excercise? Because they're doing exactly that which Anno was lashing out at: a kind of infantile mentality of retreating into fantasy instead of changing your real life for the better. Like many people who watched the series, I formed my own expectations from the first few episodes and didn't have those expectations met. While I found myself wanting to produce and share a fan-made reworking of Eva that allowed me to change things to the way I wanted, I've found myself wondering whether I should just go outside for a change instead. This thread in particular was what got me thinking: http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/11870/Alternate-Reality-in-Episode-26/ Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Drabant » Wed May 09, 2012 11:36 am Seoul Gamer wrote: Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed May 09, 2012 12:25 pm Don't all the spinoff manga (Girlfriend of Steel 2 "Angelic Days" , Shinji Ikari Raising Project, Campus Apocalypse, etc, etc) count as wish fulfillment too, not just fanworks? They're "official" AUs so they ignore a lot of what the anime and Sadamoto's manga established. Different characters are paired up or there's sexual tension, the worldview generally isn't as bleak as the series, etc, etc. As far as fanworks being looked down regardless of what series, I know some fans hate fanworks or derivative works on principle because they feel it shits on the creator's original intent. I understand where fans who think that are coming from, but personally I think fanwork or derivative works can be a great way to express your love for the series. Obviously not all of them (fanworks or AU manga) are going to be gems. I'm sure in hindsight, my own fanfiction (Eva, Sailormoon) sucks.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed May 09, 2012 12:39 pm @Seoul Gamer I'll see your "Anno lashing out" and raise a Tolkien "On Fairy-tales" -- Fiction is a nice place to visit, even if you wouldn't want to live there. We can also look around and see the answer to this one -- Seoul Gamer wrote: Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Wed May 09, 2012 3:59 pm Mr. Tines wrote: @Seoul Gamer I'll see your "Anno lashing out" and raise a Tolkien "On Fairy-tales" -- Fiction is a nice place to visit, even if you wouldn't want to live there. We can also look around and see the answer to this one -- See here (or here ) .

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed May 09, 2012 6:06 pm Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed May 09, 2012 10:51 pm Seoul Gamer wrote: Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]:             Quote Post by liquidus118 » Thu May 10, 2012 10:47 am Wish fulfilment may be too vague a term. Almost all creative pursuits are wish fulfilment of one kind or another, even if it's just the wish to write a particular kind of story. I generally find it just amusing, knowing people watched Eva, watched EoE and thought "Let's write a Shinji and Rei fanfic!" or further embraced Otaku culture because of it. Judging by what happens in Evangelion 2.0, I have a feeling Anno will use the new films, particularly 3.0, to drive home the point that he was making in EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Thu May 10, 2012 11:22 am Shinji's retreat to Eva-01 and Rei at the end of 2.0 seems like a perfect metaphor for the stereotypical basement-dweller.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu May 10, 2012 11:25 am Seoul Gamer wrote: Is the writing of fanfiction and other forms of wish fulfilment looked down on by Evangelion enthusiasts?

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Thu May 10, 2012 5:07 pm Bagheera wrote: That depends on who you ask and the works in question. Personally, I tend to look down on efforts to rewrite the story, as they tend to completely miss the point of the exercise. OTOH I'm fond of post-3I fics that look at what happens next, and bring the show's lessons full circle. We've seen the hedgehog's dilemma; what happens when the hedgehogs start talking and commit to reaching out even though it's painful? That idea complements the show's message, and there are more than a few such fics out there. Also, "what if?" fics have their place. Looking at how the show's themes might play out if certain starting elements were different is an interesting exercise, and it lets you look at those themes from a different angle. This, again, is complementary, and a worthy pursuit IMO for the philosophically inclined (or even just people who enjoy good fiction). So, like I said, it depends. There's an awful lot of crap out there, but there are some gems as well. A discriminating eye is key to getting one's fix without missing the point of the show in the process.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu May 10, 2012 5:53 pm Seoul Gamer wrote: I might as well make my intentions clear: I've basically been thinking about ways in which Third Impact could have been averted, how Rei could have survived post-Nerv and generally how things would have had to have worked out differently in order for a Shinji/Rei paring to happen in the long run. All things considered, it's a semi-impossible task. Rei II has to die in order to defeat Armisael, there's no way the pilots could have predicted the attack on Nerv or defeated the MP Evas, and it's doubtful as to whether Rei could survive without Nerv's medical care anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Fri May 11, 2012 12:25 pm Thanks, you brought up a lot of important points there. I've actually read Oedipus Ikari and thoroughly enjoyed it, along with Orchestrating The Silence, so I understand what you mean about staying true to the source material when exploring alternate/extended versions of the canon scenario. I guess the biggest issue is determining whether Rei can exist outside of Nerv, and how small changes can be made to Shinji's personality as the story goes on without derailing him.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri May 11, 2012 12:45 pm If you ask me, there's fun stuff and there's true art, and both have its merit, there's nothing wrong with a silly fantasy as long as you are aware it is one - knowing and understanding why the story is the way it it and wondering what could have been aren't necessarily mutually exclusive. I certainly wouldn't want the actual show to be like the fanfictions (nor do I think that its common perception as unbearably bleak and confusing is that accurate) but I don't see the harm in writing 'em. In many ways, the show is designed to make you wonder what could have been, because there was the potential for it to turn out better, that's why we care when it goes tumbling down, that's how we see that the characters could have done various things better. Many of the tragic parts are not so much due to ill will or being up against overwhelming odds, but something as simple as massive comunication fail or the attitude with which the protagonists faced the problem. It wouldn't have taken that much to avert certain tragedies and it is only natural that this gets people thinking, this is what we're supposed to notice.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri May 11, 2012 1:10 pm Seoul Gamer wrote: how Rei could have survived post-Nerv and generally how things would have had to have worked out differently in order for a Shinji/Rei paring to happen in the long run

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Drabant » Fri May 11, 2012 1:19 pm Mr. Tines wrote: Yui might still leave Asuka hanging out to dry vs the Eva Series.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Fri May 11, 2012 1:21 pm Mr. Tines wrote: There is a story out there where two shy reluctant mecha pilots slowly ease each other out of their shells, despite the best efforts of a brash redheaded outsider and that also conveys the moral about getting out into the big blue room. It's just not Evangelion, where the Good Soldier Ayanami can no more avoid growing to some superhuman state than Shinji could avoid breathing. If the team that took out Matarael with no outside support had continued to grow in effective small unit battle competence, things would be different with Armisael. Rei would still be down with Gendo in Terminal Dogma when things started to go down at the end, though, ready to turn into SuperGoddess; and Yui might still leave Asuka hanging out to dry vs the Eva Series.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri May 11, 2012 1:44 pm Drabant wrote: Oh come on, I really don't think Yui left Asuka out to dry. The EVA really does need someone in the pilot seat to at the very minimum act as a catalyst for it's activation.

[]: Re: Evangelion and wish fulfilment             Quote Post by Drabant » Fri May 11, 2012 1:47 pm Ornette wrote: I think he's referring to Eva-01 (Yui) waiting until after Asuka was slaughtered before, without the aid of a pilot, breaking out of the bakelite so that Shinji could have access to the entry plug. Whether it was really Yui waiting for an oppurtune time or not is up for debate.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri May 11, 2012 2:09 pm In this case, it would be a matter of staying put until Asuka's power ran out; if you take the reading that it was Yui's long plan to get herself into the centre of the 3I ritual to be able to run a Trojan on it. In a story of conspiracies within conspiracies, and with transhuman entities partaking in the game, the growing pains of a handful of teenagers (or close facsimiles thereof) are barely significant details; only relevant because how broken they are is a measure of how little interference they might be able to cause when the chips are down.
